feedback.text: I welcome the initiative of the Digital Fairness Act to strengthen consumer rights in the digital space. In this context, I would like to draw attention to an aspect that has so far received little attention but is highly relevant for European culture and digital sustainability: the preservation of digital works such as software and in particular video games as a cultural object. Video games are today an important part of European and global culture. They are not only entertainment products, but also artistic, technological and social developments. Like books, films or music, games contribute to cultural identity and reflect contemporary and creative diversity. Nevertheless, digital games are often at risk of disappearing forever when online services are shut down or when digital rights management (DRM) blocks access. A growing list of ‘dead Games’ already shows many titles that are no longer legally playable despite their purchase or licence. The loss of these works is comparable to the disappearance of films or paintings. It is all the more important that the Digital Fairness Act also takes into account the aspect of digital cultural preservation. I would therefore like to see binding mechanisms for the protection, archiving and fair accessibility of such works included in future digital consumer protection and internal market rules. This would include: 1. Right to use after acquisition: Users should also be able to access functional offline versions after discontinuing online services. 2. Obligation to maintain basic functionality: Providers of digital products should ensure that essential functionalities remain usable, in particular for content already purchased. 3. Promoting archiving & cultural preservation: Public institutions and non-profit archives should be legally enabled to also preserve and make accessible DRM-protected games. 4.Consumer protection in live service games: The handling of online games that are shut down after a few years should be regulated more transparently and fairly, especially when consumers have paid for it. The arts and culture are of high importance in Europe. The Digital Fairness Act should also reflect this claim in the digital age by protecting and fair treatment of digital cultural goods such as video games.
